37
   In Germany, this is governed by the Rundfunkgebührenstaatsvertrag (RGebStV) vom 31. August 1991,
zuletzt geändert durch Artikel 5 des Fünften Staatsvertrages zur Änderung rundfunkrechtlicher
Staatsverträge [I’m not making that up] von 06. Juli 2000 bis 07 August 2000 (Gesetz- und
Verordnungsblatt Berlin 2000, p. 447). For a popular website discussion of the powers of German
inspectors, see http://www.gezneindanke.de/faq.htm. For France, see Décret no. 92-304 du 30 mars 1992,
available at http://www.legifrance.gouv.fr./texteconsolide/FBHAD.htm; Stéphane Nerrant, La mise hors
d’usage du téléviseur et l’exigibilité de la redevance de l’audiovisuel (Intellex: 2000).
38
   For an authoritative assessment of the state of the differences, see Mirjan Damaska, Evidence Law Adrift
(New Haven: Yale U. Press, 1997), 13-14, 23-24.
39
   Figures in Hans-Jörg Albrecht, Claudia Dorsch and Christiane Krüpe, Rechtswirklichkeit und Effizienz
der Uberwachung der Telekommunikation nach den §§100a, 100b stop und andere verdeckter
Ermittlungsmaßnahmen, available at http.//www.bmj.bund.de/images/11600.pdf, p. 7. For an article
conceding that German law is theoretically more permissive but doubting that any empirical conclusions
can be drawn, see Paul Schwartz, German and U.S. Telecommunications Privacy Law: Legal Regulation of
Domestic Law En forcement Surveillance, 54 Hastings L.J. 751 (2003).

8
 European protections as yet, but nevertheless evolving in a European direction. 40 There

is certainly some truth in this: There are indeed important resemblanc es between the

systems on either side of the Atlantic. Any proper account of comparative privacy law

will have to explain many similarities as well as many differences.

Nevertheless, when all is said and done, it is impossible to ignore the fact that

Americans and Europeans are, as the Americans would put it, coming from different

places. At least as far as the law goes, we do not seem to possess general “human”

intuitions about the “horror” of privacy violations. We possess something more

complicated than that: We possess American intuitions, or as the case may be Dutch,

Italian, French or German intuitions. We must make some effort to explain this fact

before we start proclaiming universal norms of privacy protection. In particular, we will

not do justice to our transatlantic conflicts if we begin by declaring that American privacy

law has “failed” while European privacy law has “succeeded.” That is hogwash. What

we must acknowledge, instead, is that there are, on the two sides of the Atlantic, two

different cultures of privacy, which are home to different intuitive sensibilities, and which

have produced two significantly different laws of privacy.

II

So why do these sensibilities differ? Why is that French people won’t talk about

their salaries, but will take off their bikini tops? Why is that Americans comply with
